coredata.dc:title: Hierarchical ZnO/S,N:GQD composites: Biotemplated synthesis and enhanced visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity
coredata.prism:issueName: 2nd International Symposium on Energy and Environmental Photocatalytic Materials
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Aijun
Wang, Xiuping
Qi, Yanling
Ma, Zichuan
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots co-doped with sulfur and nitrogen (S,N:GQDs) are successfully combined with leaf-templated ZnO nanoparticles (L-ZnO) to obtain hierarchical L-ZnO/S,N:GQD composites exhibiting highly surface area. The morphology, structure, and the visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity are investigated. Compared with non-templated ZnO/S,N:GQDs, L-ZnO/S,N:GQD composites exhibit higher photocatalytic activity for the degradation of rhodamine B under visible light irradiation. Such elevated photocatalytic activity results from two main effects: one is the highly effective charge separation in L-ZnO/S,N:GQD composites; the other is the high surface area, allowing for efficient capture of the visible light.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO
Graphene quantum dots
Nanocomposites
Visible light
Photocatalytic activity

xocs:title-editors-group.ce:title: 2nd International Symposium on Energy and Environmental Photocatalytic Materials

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images of the S,N:GQDs. FESEM (c) and TEM (d) images of the L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.2. (e) Enlarged TEM image of the blue area in (d). (f) HRTEM image of the L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.2. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
XRD patterns of L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.1 (a), L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.2 (b), L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.5 (c), and S,N:GQDs (d).

(a) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms. (b) Pore size distribution of L-ZnO (1), L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.1 (2), L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.2 (3), L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.5 (4), and non-templated ZnO/S,N:GQDs (5). The inset in panel (b) is an enlarged view of the pore size distribution in non-templated ZnO/S,N:GQDs.
Transient photocurrent density of the sample electrodes: (1) L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.2, (2) L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.1, (3) L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.5, (4) non-templated ZnO/S,N:GQDs, and (5) L-ZnO.
Photodegradation of RhB with different samples under visible-light irradiation.
Schematic illustration of the charge transfer mechanism in L-ZnO/S,N:GQDs under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Hierarchical ZnO/S,N:GQD composites: Biotemplated synthesis and enhanced visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science and Technology, Hebei Normal University of Science & Technology, Qinhuangdao 066600, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life Science and Technology
Hebei Normal University of Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Sciences, Hebei Normal University, Shijiazhuang 050016, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots co-doped with sulfur and nitrogen (S,N:GQDs) are successfully combined with leaf-templated ZnO nanoparticles (L-ZnO) to obtain hierarchical L-ZnO/S,N:GQD composites exhibiting highly surface area. The morphology, structure, and the visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity are investigated. Compared with non-templated ZnO/S,N:GQDs, L-ZnO/S,N:GQD composites exhibit higher photocatalytic activity for the degradation of rhodamine B under visible light irradiation. Such elevated photocatalytic activity results from two main effects: one is the highly effective charge separation in L-ZnO/S,N:GQD composites; the other is the high surface area, allowing for efficient capture of the visible light.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decades, semiconductor-based photocatalysis has attracted considerable interest due to its application in environmental purification processes. Although TiO2-based photocatalysts have been regarded as one of the most promising materials [1–3], ZnO-based photocatalysts represent an appealing and suitable alternative because of the higher electron mobility and longer carrier lifetime [4]. However, the high electron-hole recombination rate in ZnO photocatalysts results in low quantum efficiencies and has hindered their use in practical applications [5–7]. The activity improvement in ZnO-based photocatalysts has been the subject of numerous studies [8–10]. The improvement strategies include elemental doping [11,12], coupling with other semiconductors [13–16], and loading of noble metal [17].

Recently, the so-called biotemplating method has attracted much attention in material science. In nature, plant components, such as rose petals [18], aquatic grass [19] and lotus leaves [20], are known to exhibit complex and hierarchical structures at the micro/nanoscale. The hierarchical structures of the leaves are thought to strongly contribute to the light capture and drive the photosynthesis process. In the materials fabrication field, these biomaterials can be used as hard templates to reproduce the morphologies and structures in inorganic nanomaterials.

In addition, the coupling of carbon materials with unique electron transport properties is an effective way to improve the photocatalytic activity of semiconductors [13]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), single or few-layer graphenes with a size less than 30nm, represent a new type of quantum dots [21,22]. Due to the remarkable electrical conductivity, tunable band gaps and extraordinary optoelectronic properties, multiple functional applications have been demonstrated with GQDs, including bioimaging [23], electrochemical biosensing [24] and photocatalysis [25].

Recent studies have shown that the optical and electrical properties of GQDs can be efficiently tuned by S and/or N doping [26,27]. In particular, visible light absorbance of GQDs can be increased by the S and/or N doping [28]. However, to the best of our knowledge, few reports have been devoted to the implementation of ZnO/S,N:GQDs for visible-light photocatalysis.

In this paper, a leaf-templated ZnO (L-ZnO) is successfully coupled with S,N:GQDs to synthesize a L-ZnO/S,N:GQD composite. L-ZnO is fabricated by using poplar leaves as a biotemplate. S,N:GQDs are successively adsorbed on the surface of the L-ZnO. After the S,N:GQDs hybridization, the visible light absorbance of the L-ZnO is significantly enhanced. The photocatalytic activity of L-ZnO/S,N:GQD composites is evaluated based on the decomposition reaction of rhodamine B complex (RhB) under visible light irradiation, showing a great improvement of the activity. The synergy between L-ZnO and S,N:GQD and the mechanisms leading to the photocatalytic activity enhancement are discussed.

Analytical grade chemicals were used as received without further purification. Glutaraldehyde (Tianjin Bo Di Chemical Co., Ltd), hydrochloric acid (Tianjin Yong Da Chemical Reagent Development Center, 99%), and zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 98%, Aldrich) were used for the preparation of the L-ZnO. Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) conducting glass substrates were purchased from Asahi Glass Company, Japan.

S,N:GQDs were synthesized using a modified procedure reported in a previous paper [28]. Citric acid (13.1mmol) and thiourea (39.3mmol) were dissolved in deionized water (60mL) and transferred into a Teflon autoclave heated at 160°C for 18h. The obtained S,N:GQDs were then separated by centrifugation, followed by several washings with absolute alcohol. Finally, S,N:GQDs were obtained through vacuum freeze drying.

Fresh poplar leaves were treated with 2% glutaraldehyde/phosphate buffered saline (PBS; pH=7.2) solution at 4°C for 6h to promote fixation of cells and tissues. The samples were successively rinsed with 0.2mol/L PBS solution and pure water. The leaves were then immersed in 5% HCl solution for 13h at room temperature. After rinsing with pure water, the leaves were placed in a 0.8M Zn(NO3)2·6H2O solution in an oven at 60°C for 72h. The samples were then rinsed with pure water and dried at 60°C. Finally, the L-ZnO was obtained by calcination in air at 500°C with a temperature ramping rate of 1°Cmin−1 and tempered for 2h.

Typically, 0.06g of L-ZnO was added to 30mL of S,N:GQD solution. The mixture was then stirred at room temperature for 24h. After rinsing with water several times, L-ZnO/S,N:GQD composites were collected. The obtained samples were denoted as L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-x, where x represents the S,N:GQD solution concentration, i.e. 0.1, 0.2 or 0.5mg/mL.

The common ZnO was obtained by directly calcinating the Zn(NO3)2·6H2O. Then, 0.06g of the common ZnO was mixed with 0.2mg/mL of S,N:GQDs solution. After washing with water, the non-templated ZnO/S,N:GQDs were obtained.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected on a Bruker D8-ADVANCE X-ray powder diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images were recorded on a Hitachi S4800 electron microscope. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained on a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on an ESCALAB 250 instrument. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area and Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) analysis were carried out using a Micromeritics 3Flex surface characterization analyzer. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were studied using a Hitachi U-4100 spectrophotometer. BaSO4 was taken as a reference sample. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using a Hitachi F-4600 florescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 325nm. Analyses were always performed at room temperature.

Electrochemical analysis was conducted using CHI–660B electrochemical system with a conventional three-electrode cell configuration with Pt wire as a counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode. The electrolyte solution was 0.1M Na2SO4. The working electrode was prepared on FTO glass (13mm×10mm) as follows: the FTO glass was firstly cleaned by sonication in acetone for 30min and dried at 353K. Then, the boundary of FTO glass was covered with an insulating tape, with the exposed effective area of 1cm2. Ten milligram of the products were dispersed in 2mL ethanol and then ground to obtain a slurry. The slurry was then spread onto the pretreated FTO glass and dried at 393K. The variation of the photoinduced current density with time (i–t curve) was measured at a bias potential of 0V (vs SCE) over five cycles during which light was switched on and off. A 500W Xe lamp with a D300 monochromator was used for the visible light irradiation. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were carried out in the frequency range from 0.1 to 106 Hz.

The photocatalytic activity of the samples was investigated by measuring the RhB dye degradation under visible light irradiation. A 500W Xe lamp with a 420nm cutoff filter was used as a visible light source. Before irradiation, 30mg of catalysts were dispersed in 40mL RhB aqueous solution (5mg/L) and magnetically stirred in the dark for 30min to attain the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The mixture was then exposed to the light source placed at 15cm distance. At regular irradiation steps, 4mL aliquots of the suspension were withdrawn and subjected to centrifugation. The RhB concentration was monitored through the UV–vis absorption at a wavelength of 553nm. The RhB degradation efficiency (D%) is defined as:
(1) D%=(1−C/C0)×100%
where C and C0 represent the RhB concentration at time t and the equilibrium concentration of dye, respectively.

The yellow-green S,N:GQD aqueous solution presents a homogeneous phase (Fig. S1, in Supplementary information). The morphology of S,N:GQDs studied by TEM shows a fairly uniform distribution of GQDs with diameters of about 6–10nm (Fig. 1 a). A representative HRTEM result indicates a lattice spacing of 0.24nm, which agrees well with the (1120) plane of graphite (Fig. 1b) [28]. The presence of the S and N elements is confirmed by XPS (Fig. S2). Due to the doping with sulfur, the S,N:GQDs show absorption bands at 335 and 595nm (Fig. S3). A typical FESEM image of the L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.2 composites is shown in Fig. 1c. Interestingly, the composite exhibits a hierarchical structure and flower-like shape, mainly composed of multiple sheet-like aggregates. This result confirms that during the slow calcination process, the original leaf template is transferred to the ZnO. The morphology is further confirmed by TEM investigations (Fig. 1d). As observed in high magnification TEM images (Fig. 1e), each sheet is composed of multiple quasi-spherical ZnO nanoparticles with size between 10 and 20nm. HRTEM image confirms the combination of ZnO and S,N:GQDs, as shown in Fig. 1f. The lattice spacing of 0.26 and 0.35nm corresponds to the (002) plane of hexagonal ZnO and the (002) graphite facet, respectively. Compared with the L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.2 composites, the non-templated ZnO/S,N:GQDs exhibit irregular block-like shapes, as shown in Fig. S4.

The XRD patterns of the S,N:GQDs and L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-x composites are shown in Fig. 2 . S,N:GQDs exhibit a very broad diffraction peak centered at about 23.6°. The diffraction peaks related to ZnO are assigned to the hexagonal wurtzite structure (JCPDS file no. 36-1451) [29]. The series of L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-x samples present the similar XRD patterns, while the peaks of S,N:GQDs are not observed due to the low content of S,N:GQDs in the composites.

XPS spectroscopy was conducted for composition and chemical analysis of L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.2, as shown in Fig. 3 . As shown in Fig. 3a, the XPS survey spectrum exhibits the signals from Zn 2p, O 1s, N 1s, S 2p, and C 1s. XPS high resolution core level spectra are shown in Fig. 3b–f. The Zn 2p spectrum (Fig. 3b) is composed of one doublet peak centered at 1021.8eV (Zn 2p3/2) and 1044.9eV (Zn 2p1/2). The high-resolution O 1s spectrum shows two peaks at 530.7 and 532eV (Fig. 3c) which are attributed to the Zn-O binding [17] and adsorbed hydroxyl species [30], respectively. The N 1s peak XPS spectrum shows two peaks at 399.2eV and 400.2eV (Fig. 3d) which are attributed to the pyrrolic N (CNC) and graphitic N or NH bands [28,31], respectively. The S 2p XPS spectrum shows two peaks at 163.1 and 164.3eV, which are assigned to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of thiophene, respectively [28]. As presented in Fig. 3f, C 1s spectrum exhibits three peaks at 284.7eV, 285.7eV, and 288.1eV. The peak at 284.7eV is assigned to sp2C in graphene [32]; the peak at 285.7eV is assigned to sp3C in CN, C-S and CO, and the peak at 288.1eV corresponds to CO from carbonyls and carboxylates [33].

The optical property of the samples was investigated by UV–vis absorption (Fig. 4 ). The L-ZnO exhibits strong absorption in the ultraviolet region due to the transition of excited state electrons from the valence band to the conduction band under UV illumination. Compared with the L-ZnO, the L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-x composites show higher absorption in the visible light region, which is attributed to the S,N:GQDs coupling. The non-templated ZnO/S,N:GQDs also exhibit strong visible light absorption, further confirming that the S,N:GQDs enhance the visible light absorbance. These results indicate a strong interaction between L-ZnO and S,N:GQDs, which contributes to the photocatalytic activity improvement under visible light irradiation. The combination of L-ZnO and S,N:GQDs changes the color of the composites, as shown in Fig. S5. With increase in the amount of S,N:GQDs, the L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-x composites become darker in color. Additionally, the PL spectra is also strongly affected by the combination of ZnO with S,N:GQDs (Fig. S6). The L-ZnO sample exhibits the highest PL emission intensity; the PL intensity is reduced when ZnO is combined with S,N:GQDs, indicating a higher charge separation efficiency of photoexcited electrons and holes [34–37].

The N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms, and pore sized distribution results are shown in Fig. 5 . As shown in Fig. 5a, the pattern obtained in the non-templated ZnO/S,N:GQDs can be divided into type with a type H3 hysteresis loop, according to IUPAC classification [38]. Non-templated ZnO/S,N:GQDs have a relatively low hysteresis loop due to the low surface area (0.86m2/g, Table S1). The pore size distribution (Fig. 5b) indicates the coexistence of microporous and mesoporous structures. L-ZnO and L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-x display a similar type with a type H3 hysteresis loop, which reveals the presence of mesopores. The mesoporous structure is further confirmed by the pore size distribution analysis shown in Fig. 5b. Compared with that of the non-templated ZnO/S,N:GQDs, the BET surface area of L-ZnO is much higher and estimated to be 32.34m2/g. This result shows that the hierarchical biotemplates play an important role in improving the surface areas of the composites. After combination with the S,N:GQDs, the BET surface areas of the L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.1, −0.2 and −0.5 are improved to 39.55, 44.14 and 41.58m2/g, respectively (Table S1), higher than that of the L-ZnO. Particularly, compared with the non-templated ZnO/S,N:GQDs, L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-x composites exhibit a significantly higher BET surface areas, which contributes to the photocatalytic activity improvement. It is noted that increasing the amount of the added S,N:GQDs from 0.2 to 0.5mg/mL does not further improve the surface areas of the composites. The decrease in BET surface area with x is explained by the higher covering of the S,N:GQDs, which agrees with the photocatalytic results discussed later.

To further investigate the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes, the photoelectrochemical performances of the L-ZnO, non-templated ZnO/S,N:GQDs, and series of L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-x photoeletrodes were studied using photoinduced i–t curves, as shown in Fig. 6 . It can be seen that the L-ZnO shows almost no photocurrent response. On the other hand, a steady and prompt photocurrent generation is observed in the non-templated ZnO/S,N:GQDs and L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-x composites under on-off illumination cycles, indicating an enhanced photoinduced separation of electron/hole pairs [39,40]. All of the L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-x composites exhibit a higher photocurrent density than the non-templated ZnO/S,N:GQDs. The photocurrent generation of the L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-x composites increases along with the increase in amount of added S,N:GQDs, from 0.1 to 0.2mg/mL. However, further increase in amount of added S,N:GQDs results in decrease in the photogenerated current density. These results are likely due to the enhanced light absorption of the composites in visible region.

EIS is a useful tool to study the photogenerated charge separation process [39]. EIS Nyquist plots of the sample electrodes under visible light irradiation are shown in Fig. S7. The arc radius on EIS Nyquist plots of series of L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-x composites are smaller than those of the non-templated ZnO/S,N:GQDs and L-ZnO. These results suggest the effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and fast interfacial charge transfer in the prepared L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-x composites [41]. Hence, a higher rate in the photocatalytic activity of L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-x composites would be achieved.

The photocatalytic activity of the different photocatalysts for the removal of RhB under visible light irradiation was investigated (Fig. 7 ). RhB dye is stable under visible light and cannot be degraded in the absence of a photocatalyst. Under visible light irradiation for 240min, no evident RhB degradation is observed when in the presence of the L-ZnO, indicating that L-ZnO has no photocatalytic activity. However, through the coupling with S,N:GQDs, the photocatalytic activity of the L-ZnO is significantly improved. After the same photoirradiation period, with L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.1, −0.2 and −0.5 catalysts, RhB was degraded by 66.6%, 82.4%, and 71.7%, respectively. The photocatalytic activity is higher than that of the non-templated ZnO/S,N:GQDs for which degradation reaches 54.1%. Interestingly, L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.2 exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity, and the degradation degree of RhB does not increase when the S,N:GQDs amount is further increased.

A recycling experiment was performed for studying the photodegradation of the L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.2. As shwon in Fig. S8, the catalyst still exhibits high photocatalytic activity after the three cycles, indicating a good stability.

Fig. 8 shows the possible reaction mechanism of the prepared composites under visible-light irradiation. Based on above results, we conclude that the photocatalytic activity of the L-ZnO/S,N:GQD composites is attributed to two factors. The first main factor is an efficient charge separation and high electron photogeneration efficiency. ZnO is a wide band gap semiconductor and it cannot be excited by photons with light wavelengths longer than 400nm in visible light region. The conduction band (CB) and the valence band (VB) of the ZnO are 4.2 and 7.6eV, respectively [42], and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of GQDs are 1.8–2.8 and 5.1–5.4eV [43], respectively. Under visible light irradiation, the electrons in S,N:GQDs are injected to the CB of L-ZnO. Therefore, the efficient charge separation is obtained [44,45]. The electrons can reduce the oxygen absorbed on the catalyst to O2 − radicals, whereas the holes can oxidize the absorbed H2O to produce OH radicals [46,47]. The O2 − and OH radicals are the reactive species, which are responsible for the degradation of RhB. The other factor responsible for high photocatalytic activity is the high surface area of the composites. Using the biotemplates, the prepared L-ZnO/S,N:GQDs exhibit high surface area as compared to non-templated ZnO/S,N:GQD composites, which can contribute to efficient capture of the visible light during the photocatalysis [48]. Additionally, from the N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms, it is observed that the L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.2 exhibits higher BET surface areas, compared with those of L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.1 and L-ZnO/S,N:GQD-0.5. This result further confirms the important effect of hierarchical structures with higher surface areas.

In summary, new hierarchical L-ZnO/S,N:GQD composites have been synthesized by using poplar leaves as biotemplates. The visible light absorbance of ZnO is shown to be red-shifted, due to the S,N:GQD coupling. Compared with the non-templated ZnO/S,N:GQDs, the L-ZnO/S,N:GQD composites exhibit higher photocatalytic activity for the degradation of RhB dyes. The likely reaction mechanisms are proposed and the enhanced photocatalytic activity could be attributed to two factors: the highly efficient charge separation of electron-hole pairs in composites, and the higher surface area that can efficiently capture the visible light and improve the efficiency of visible light absorption.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51402087), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (C2014407008), the Program for the Top Young Talents of Higher Learning Institutions of Hebei Province (BJ2014026), and the PhD research Startup Foundation of Hebei University of Science & Technology, China (2014YB002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2016.06.113.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
